content.problemStatement:
Chef has recently started conducting a masterclass where he teaches his disciples world-class recipes. There are NNN students in his class, standing in a line represented by a string SSS of length NNN. Each character in SSS is either `B` (representing a boy) or `G` (representing a girl).

Chef has an annoyance factor KKK. He gets annoyed and refuses to teach if and only if there are at least KKK consecutive disciples of the same gender in the lineup.

You are allowed to perform **at most** one operation: in a single operation, you may reverse any substring of SSS.

Determine whether it is possible to perform at most one such operation to obtain a string in which Chef does not get annoyed, so he ends up teaching his disciples.

Note that a substring is obtained by deleting some (possible zero) characters from the beginning and some (possibly zero) characters from the end of the string.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two integers NNN and KKK — the length of SSS and the annoyance factor of Chef respectively.
The next line contains the string SSS.




Output Format
For each test case, print `YES` on a new line if it is possible to perform at most one operation to obtain a string in which Chef does not get annoyed and ends up teaching his disciples, otherwise print `NO` on a new line.


You may print each character of the string in uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings `YES`, `yEs`, `yes`, and `yeS` will all be treated as identical).
constraints: SSS is a string, contains only the characters `B` and `G`.
The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.input:
6
7 2
BGBGBGB
3 2
GGB
8 3
BBBBGGGG
4 2
GGGB
8 4
BBBBGBBB
9 4
BBBBGBBBG
sampleTestCases.output:
YES
YES
YES
NO
NO
YES
sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
In the first test case, no operation is needed since there are no adjacent characters that are the same.


In the second test case, you can choose to operate on the substring from the second to the third character. Now, SSS becomes GBG, and there are no adjacent characters that are the same.


In the third test case, you can choose to operate on the substring from the third to the sixth character. Now, SSS becomes BBGGBBGG, and there are no three consecutive characters that are the same.


In the fourth test case, there is no way to prevent Chef from being annoyed.

hidden.input:
6

3 2

9 4
